coredata.dc:title: A hybrid data-driven method for voltage state prediction and fault warning of Li-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yufeng
Gong, Xuejian
Lin, Zhiyu
Xu, Lei
coredata.dc:description: As the extensive application of electrochemical energy storage (EES), Li-ion battery fault is a key factor reference to the reliable operation and system security, influencing by the environment temperature and battery voltage. To address distinct challenges in lithium-ion battery fault prediction, such as nonlinearity and complex electrochemical reactions within battery state sequence data, a novel 1DCNN-Bi-LSTM hybrid network has been proposed to predict the Li-ion battery fault. Firstly, an 1DCNN module is introduced to extract voltage-related multi-dimension features. Secondly, a Bi-LSTM module is used to learn long-term dependence relationships among fused features while integrating a self-attention mechanism. To further verify the algorithm's effectiveness, a new 18650 battery dataset has been set up under various conditions between day and night. The experimental results show that our model has high accuracy and exemplary performance in various environmental temperatures. The prediction errors for comparative experiments are approximately MAPE of 0.03 %, RMSE of 0.0003 %, MAE of 0.12 %, and R2 of 0.99. Compared with mainstream methods, our prediction result is close to true values, performs better at peaks and valleys, and has higher computational efficiency. Considering the temperature factor and voltage variation, our developed method can be effectively applied to battery management system (BMS).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion batteries
1DCNN-Bi-LSTM
Voltage state prediction
Fault warning

head.ce:title: A hybrid data-driven method for voltage state prediction and fault warning of Li-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic and Information Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Shenyang Fire Science and Technology Research Institute of MEM, Shenyang, 110034, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenyang Fire Science and Technology Research Institute of MEM

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the extensive application of electrochemical energy storage (EES), Li-ion battery fault is a key factor reference to the reliable operation and system security, influencing by the environment temperature and battery voltage. To address distinct challenges in lithium-ion battery fault prediction, such as nonlinearity and complex electrochemical reactions within battery state sequence data, a novel 1DCNN-Bi-LSTM hybrid network has been proposed to predict the Li-ion battery fault. Firstly, an 1DCNN module is introduced to extract voltage-related multi-dimension features. Secondly, a Bi-LSTM module is used to learn long-term dependence relationships among fused features while integrating a self-attention mechanism. To further verify the algorithm's effectiveness, a new 18650 battery dataset has been set up under various conditions between day and night. The experimental results show that our model has high accuracy and exemplary performance in various environmental temperatures. The prediction errors for comparative experiments are approximately MAPE of 0.03 %, RMSE of 0.0003 %, MAE of 0.12 %, and R2 of 0.99. Compared with mainstream methods, our prediction result is close to true values, performs better at peaks and valleys, and has higher computational efficiency. Considering the temperature factor and voltage variation, our developed method can be effectively applied to battery management system (BMS).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

The weighting factor for
h ⃐
t
(backward transmission)

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, electrochemical energy storage (EES) has become a main development area in the current energy storage industry. With short construction cycle, convenient layout and fast response speed, EES has widely applicated in the electric, industry, and residence fields. In addition, EES performance [1,2] has been determined by the Li-ion batteries self, and the battery fault may decay EES useful life and even lead to thermal runaway issue. As the battery-operated devices increase sharply, the safety and reliability of Li-ion batteries have attracted more and more attention [3]. Through monitoring the Li-ion batteries' state, new EES can warn failure and reduce the thermal runaway caused by battery failure [4–7]. Usually, a short circuit caused by mechanical or electrical abuse raises the cell temperature [8]. The elevated temperature can melt the diaphragm and bring much severe short circuit, leading to worse thermal runaway. In addition to safety issues, battery faults can affect the EES performance [9,10]. As known in Refs. [11–13], battery fault detection by monitoring temperature is a basis for short circuit or thermal runaway warnings. Although these methods are still widely used, the battery fault couldn't be detected quickly due to the slow thermal response in the battery device. Analyzing the battery's voltage signal is a fast method for detecting battery faults. Many researchers have also utilized voltage to detect battery faults, such as internal and external short circuits [14–16], overcharging and over-discharge faults [17]. These methods validate the voltage signals importance in battery fault detection.

This paper uses the battery datasets as input information and proposes a data-driven battery fault prediction method based on the time series prediction network. The A123 dataset from the University of Maryland [18,19] and self-made 18650 battery dataset have been employed as input. By combining one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1DCNN), self-attention mechanism and bi-directional long short-term memory (Bi-LSTM), the battery fault prediction method has been proposed. In this method, the charge/discharge state, voltage, battery current, and temperature has been used as model input to predict the battery voltage change. While the battery voltage prediction model is constructed, the battery fault is predicted by analyzing the voltage value and its change rate. Based on 1DCNN-Bi-LSTM network, a battery fault prediction method has been proposed that is suitable for multi-temperature conditions, and the contributions are summarized as follows.
(1)
1DCNN is introduced to capture various parameter features, and then Bi-LSTM is used to extract the inverse and forward features for the data sequence, which can be utilized to improve prediction accuracy. Finally, a self-attention mechanism module is employed to capture the global information, in order to make the prediction network more robust under multi-temperature conditions.
(2)
To verify the algorithm effectiveness, a new 18650 battery dataset has been set up under different conditions between day and night, enriching the temperature range for training and testing data.
(3)
By calculating MAPE, RMSE and MAE, the experimental results show that our method has smaller errors and faster prediction under multiple temperature conditions. Our algorithm can distinguish different types of voltage faults by setting different voltage thresholds and corresponding warning alarm levels.

In the following sections, Section 2 reviews the related literature. Section 3 describes the utilized datasets and the proposed network. Then, Section 4 presents the experimental results and its detailed discussion. Finally, Section 5 concludes the paper and discusses the limitations of the proposed method.
Battery fault prediction methods can be generally classified into four categories [20]: model-based methods, signal processing-based methods, knowledge-based methods, and data-driven methods.

The model-based method [21] determines whether a fault occurs in the system by mathematically modeling the battery state. Obtaining the fault parameters, the model-based ways usually utilize a set of residual signals, and then comparing them with the fault threshold. The model-based method mainly includes two steps of residual generation and residual evaluation, among which there are three residual generation: state estimation method [22], parameter estimation method [23], and cosmological space method [24].

The signal processing-based method [25] is considered that the cell voltage should obey a normal distribution in the battery system. The voltage output of each battery terminal should be approximately equal, which provide that the cells are well aligned. When a battery fault occurs, the data collected by the sensor can execute the fault information. Fault analysis in time domain analysis [4] can be performed directly from the test data. A spectral analysis in the frequency domain can obtain amplitude and phase frequency characteristics of test data. Typical methods include the Fourier [26,27] and Wavelet transform [28,29].

The knowledge-based method [30] utilizes the battery system knowledge, particularly suitable for nonlinear and complex system [31,32]. Knowledge-based method [33] contains graph theory, fuzzy logic, and expert system methods [20]. Its knowledge and rules are constructed through historical databases, as well as the rich experience of domain experts. Although the working principle and diagnostic results are easy to explain, further researches are still needed on the fault mechanism when they are applied to fault diagnosis.

When the battery specification and application scenario change, those above modeling approaches maybe disabled [34], which is challenging to apply in practice. As the cell voltages should follow a specific distribution under the same conditions [35,36], the data-driven methods have been a new research topic. Based on information entropy [37], local outlier [38], and correlation coefficient [39], the data-driven methods can accurately detect abnormal data and diagnose fault occurs. However, the above methods cannot determine the specific fault type. The machine learning methods have obtained triumph in battery sate prediction field, such as state of charge (SOC) [3], remaining useful life (RUL) [40] and so on. Currently, many machine learning methods are also applied to Li-ion battery fault prediction [41,42]. Machine learning algorithms establish a fuzzy inference relationship between data feature and specific fault types, enhancing algorithms to predict the battery fault type precisely.

In this paper, we propose a temporal learning-based method for battery fault prediction, which combines self-made and public datasets to complete the verification experiments during training process and improves algorithms robustness under multi-temperature conditions.

For the experiments, the first dataset is the A123 battery data from the University of Maryland, which is a Lithium-Iron-Phosphate (LiFePO4) battery data. The technical specifications of this battery cell are shown in Table 1 . The rated capacity of this battery is 1100 mAh, the rated voltage is 3.3 V, the upper/lower cut-off voltage is 3.6 V/2.0 V, and the maximum continuous discharge current is 30 A. The self-made dataset has been established using the 18650 (LiFePO4) battery charge-discharge cycles under the day and night conditions. Its rated voltage is 3.7 V, the upper/lower cut-off voltage is 4.2 V/2.75 V. The technical specifications are also presented in Table 1.

Due to the battery pack inconsistency, the charging control technology fails to accurately regulate and control the problem, thus some single cells inevitably appear overcharge phenomenon. This overcharge condition manifests as a rapid increase in battery voltage with the rise in polarization, resulting in lower battery capacity, higher internal temperature, and even the explosion risk. Similar to the overcharge phenomenon, the over-discharge phenomenon is manifested as the battery voltage is lower than the rated value still continues to discharge, making the battery voltage below the rated value. It leads to a rapid decline in capacity and a rapid increase in internal resistance, and the battery gradually fails [43,44]. Its phenomenon can also trigger the sudden fault of battery internal short circuit, thermal runaway, the battery fault associated with these abnormal phenomena, as shown in Table 2 . Therefore, the accurate prediction of voltage abnormalities can effectively prevent the voltage faults in Li-ion batteries operation. Table 3 shows the voltage thresholds for this paper's voltage anomaly warning system. It displays the alarm thresholds for three levels of voltage faults and their correspondence.

The above tables show three levels of voltage warning thresholds: Low, Medium and High. Low level warning indicates that the voltage has abnormal values but still safe. Medium level warning means the voltage abnormal value is dangerous, and needs to check the fault location and cause. High level warning shows that it is very dangerous and should be moved away from the current environment in time to avoid the danger.

The proposed battery fault prediction network contains a feature extractor and a state prediction module, named 1DCNN-Bi-LSTM network. First, the battery dataset has been used as input, which is min-max normalized in the data processing network. Then the processed data are transferred to the 1DCNN network, and each parameter information (voltage, voltage change rate, current, and temperature) of the Li-ion battery dataset is extracted respectively. The accumulated data are then input to the state prediction network. The network learns the battery voltage change characteristics of Li-ion battery during charging and discharging based on the previously obtained information to predict the battery voltage state accurately. It then evaluates the predicted voltage value with the voltage threshold to determine whether the current battery is faulty or not. The proposed network structure is shown in Fig. l (a).

The flow of battery voltage fault prediction system can be described in detail. First, we preprocess the battery data as the model input. The pre-processing operation generally includes: Processing the abnormal values in the data, and then normalizing the data to ensure its availability.

Secondly, the prediction model is designed for the training process. 1DCNN can effectively and rapidly extract different scales features from time series data, while Bi-LSTM can capture long-term dependencies in time series, and self-attention module can obtain global information covering the time series. By combining them together, local features extracted by 1DCNN, global information enhanced via self-attention module and long-term dependencies captured through Bi-LSTM can be utilized to more comprehensively describe the structure and patterns. Considering that 1DCNN can process input sequences in parallel, combining 1DCNN and Bi-LSTM can accelerate the training process. What's more, self-attention module can adjust weight parameter by global feature, which improve training efficiency and reduce overfitting risks.

Finally, setting the voltage threshold when the fault occurs, and then inputting the battery data collected in real time during the battery operation. The battery data collected in real time during battery operation are input into the model to predict future battery voltage accordingly. The prediction results are input to the fault occurrence detection module to determine whether there is a fault. If there is a fault, the model makes a judgment and warning in advance. In conclusion, the proposed tactics improve the model's generalization ability, and well adapt to different data distributions and fault modes. The training parameters of our designed network are shown as Table 4 .

Convolutional neural network (CNN) is mainly used for pattern recognition and feature extraction, which is widely used in the computer vision field. In the convolutional layer, the filter performs feature extraction by using appropriate window size and sliding step. Then the high-level features are averaged in the pooling layer and output to the fully connected layer for regression prediction or classification. In this paper, we use the 1DCNN network to extract the battery variation features during the charging and discharging processes. As seen in Fig. 1 (a), four independent 1DCNNs are used to extract the parameter change features of voltage, voltage change ratio, current and temperature during battery operation, respectively. Thus, the model could better study the battery parameters during operation and thus predict the battery voltage more accurately. The 1DCNN structure is illustrated in Fig. 1 (b).

Meanwhile, the fault prediction of Li-ion batteries is generally a continuous evolution process dependent on the battery usage time. To retain the temporal information, the proposed 1DCNN networks is followed with a BatchNorm layer, a ReLU layer and a MaxPooling layer to better extract the battery feature parameters and prevent the overfitting issue. The network parameters of the 1DCNN structure are listed in Table 5 .

The data processing procedure for the 1DCNN modular can be described in the following parts. First, the preprocessed voltage data enters a one-dimensional convolutional layer, which extracts features by moving the filter along the time axis. The process of feature extraction [45] is as follows:
(1)
S l
= f
(
∑
i = 1
N
C K
X 0
∗
K i
+
b i l
)
where X 0 is the input data, K
i is the i-th convolution kernel, S
l is the feature map of the l-th layer, f(·) is the activation function, N
CK is the number of convolution kernels used in the convolution layer, b l
i is the i-th bias of the l-th feature map, ∗ is the convolution operation. Following with the Batch Normalization layer, the network improves the training speed and model performance. The model uses ReLU as the activation function.

MaxPooling is responsible for preventing overfitting and improving the robustness. Thus, the extracted feature sequences are input to the fully connected layer, and the features are dimensionally reduced and mapped to the sample space. Finally, the processed data are inputs to the SoftMax layer to obtain the Bi-LSTM input for subsequent data prediction.

Due to its design characteristics, the long short-term memory (LSTM) is well suited for modeling time series dataset compared to other network models. Also, LSTM models can learn what information is remembered and other is forgotten through the training process. The dependencies that affect battery voltage changes over more extended time series can be better captured using LSTM models. Traditional one-way LSTM networks may lose some key battery changes when processing time series, especially in the predicting long time series. Bi-LSTM can reduce information loss and improve model performance by simultaneously considering past and future information, thus alleviating the vanishing gradients problem. Bi-LSTM capture voltage and current variation from both past and future time steps simultaneously. Forward LSTM processes past sequences, while backward LSTM deals with future sequences. This enables the network to have a more comprehensive understanding of contextual information in time series, which helps improve its ability to predict future trends. The Bi-LSTM neural network structure model has two independent LSTM models. The hidden state of Bi-LSTM is divided into forward-order and inverse-order. The forward-order state refers to the current state depending on the hidden state of the previous moment, and the inverse-order state refers to the current state depending on the hidden state of the later time step.

Therefore, Bi-LSTM can extract the inverse features and improve prediction accuracy. Each layer [46] can be defined as:
(2)
h ⇀
t
= H
(
w
x
h ⇀
x t
+
w
h ⇀
h ⇀

+
b

)
h ⃐
t
= H
(
w
x
h ⃐
x t
+

h ⃐

y t
= G
(
w
h ⇀
y
h ⇀
t
+
w
h ⃐
y
h ⃐
t
+
b y
)
here H(·) and G(·) denote the activation function, which can be chosen according to the situation. H(·) chooses tanh(·) function, as well as G(·) is sigmoid(·) function.

The battery voltage feature sequences to the Bi-LSTM model are input to two LSTM neural networks in forward and reverse order for feature extraction, after which the two output vectors (i.e., the extracted feature vectors) are stitched together to obtain a battery voltage vector as the final feature representation.
The training process of Bi-LSTM is similar to traditional recurrent neural network (RNN), optimizing model parameters through backpropagation algorithm and gradient descent.

In terms of loss function, cross entropy loss function [47] is usually used, as shown below:
(3)
L = −
1 N
∑
n = 1
N
∑
t = 1
T n
(
y t
( n )

( n )
+
(

y t
( n )
)

y ˆ
t
( n )
)
)
where N is the number of training samples, and T n means the sequence length of the n-th sample.
y t
( n )
ˆ
( )
represents the true label of the n-th sample at time step t, and
y ˆ
t
( n )
is defined as the corresponding predicted label.

The Bi- LSTM network structure is illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). The C
t is the input and h
t denotes the cell state, σ means the sigmoid function at time step t. Compared to the LSTM, Bi-LSTM model can encode the time series information from backward to forward. The modular allows the voltage prediction module to learn the voltage changes and other related parameters in both before and after the voltage anomalies appearance, thus enabling a more accurate grasp of voltage fault appearance. Our proposed model has better efficiency and performance than the single LSTM structure model in feature extraction. Therefore, the Bi-LSTM model can better capture the time series dependence in both directions. Based on this, the main body of State prediction module is a Bi-LSTM network, which allows the network to learn more about the battery voltage characteristics and thus obtain better prediction results.

Alleviating the vanishing gradient problem, LSTM networks are commonly used to handle long sequences, but may encounter problems such as vanishing gradients or exploding gradients during training. However, the traditional LSTM networks may lose some key variation of battery when processing long time cycle series. Thus, importing.

Bi- LSTM network structure is considering past and future information and improving the model performance. Bi-LSTM can simultaneously propagate gradients in both forward and backward propagation, thereby alleviating the vanishing gradient problem to a certain extent and helping to improve the training stability. Bidirectional processing enables Bi-LSTM to obtain richer comprehensive representation of information changes at each time step. This helps the network better understand the complex structure and patterns, thereby improving the prediction accuracy.

By considering the bidirectional information, Bi-LSTM can better generalize the unknown data information, rather than the data only exists in the training set. This makes the model more adaptable and robust, and can better cope with various time series prediction tasks. The Bi-LSTM parameters used for the network are in Table 6 .

The self-attention mechanism enables models to focus on important regions over a larger range than traditional methods. In this paper, the self-attention mechanism is used to enhance the predictive capability of Bi-LSTM networks and to capture both global and focused information. Global features contain the battery information among the entire time series, which helps the network to capture the battery long-term dependencies, and understand the evolution trend and periodic changes. The use of global features can reduce the overfitting risk on training data at specific times. Therefore, the model can better understand the correlation and influence factors between information changes, thereby improving the prediction accuracy. What's more, in order to get more accurate prediction information, the rest parameters (such as current, temperature and so on) need to be considered. The self-attention mechanism also captures the specific changes of other parameters (such as current and temperature) to enhance the prediction value. This allows us to deal the voltage changes along its related parameters through the self-attention mechanism. Using the self-attention can also better help us filter the unimportant battery parameter features and avoid the interference of other irrelevant features, thus achieving the computational resources while improving the prediction accuracy.

The training processes have been performed on a graphic workstation equipped with computer CPU for Intel (R) Core (TM) i5-7200U CPU @2.5 GHz and one NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs. The software is pycharm2022.1.3, and API is TensorFlow as well as the Python environment used is Python 3.8.

The A123 battery dataset contains battery charging and discharging data (voltage, current, temperature) at ambient temperatures ranging from −10 °C to 50 °C. The 18650 battery dataset contains battery charging and discharging data at ambient temperatures of 4 °C and 13.5 °C. The C-rate is a measurement of the current during charging or discharging of a battery relative to its nominal capacity. The adopted charging strategy follows these steps: Constant current charging at 0.5C until reaching the 4.2V cut-off voltage during the process, and then constant voltage charging until the charging current reduces to 0.05C, at which point the charge is terminated. The discharging strategy entails discharging at a constant current of 0.5C until reaching the 2.75V cut-off voltage, upon which discharging ceases. We divide each dataset into training set, test set and validation set in the ratio of 6:2:2.

The MSE values of the validation set for the network training are shown in Fig. 2 (a). Our neural network attains peak performance in terms of MSE at approximately epoch 40, as depicted in the left plot. It is evident from the figure that the MSE has been reduced to a lower range and the curve has plateaued, leading us to designate 64 epochs as the optimal training cycle for achieving minimal MSE with the neural network. To prove that this method can effectively predict the voltage profile, Fig. 2 (b) shows the voltage and its prediction curve at a temperature of 20 °C. The curves display that the network can accurately predict the battery voltage, and the predicted values are very close to the actual value. Fig. 2 (c) shows the error histogram between the true and predicted values at 20 °C temperature, from which it can be seen that most errors are within a very small range, proving the accuracy of the proposed method. In addition, when the change curve of battery voltage is smoother, the prediction accuracy is higher theoretically, which ensures the model feasibility and reliability.

We can set different prediction steps to get the prediction results of different time lengths. While the time length to be predicted is longer, the prediction accuracy is lower, so we need to choose the appropriate prediction step to achieve the best prediction results. The prediction performance of three different time-invariant cases is considered, named as time_step_10, time_step_20 and time_step_30. The MAE, MSE values are presented in Table 7 . The length of the input time series is 7,084, and the time step size is 10, 20, 30 for each experiment separately.

The coefficients of determination (R2) for the network model are seen in Table 8 . Through the derived regression coefficients, it can be seen that the fitting degree between the predicted and the true value is large, indicating that the predicted value explains the true value to a high degree. It also reflects that the network can get better prediction accuracy. The regression relationship between the predicted and true values at 20 °C is shown in Fig. 2 (d).

Many mainstream methods can obtain relatively good results from the above experimental results at lower temperatures, but the prediction accuracy decreases significantly along the temperature increases. The possible reason is that at high temperatures, the internal changes within the battery is more active, leading to larger variations in the battery voltage. The temperature is positive correlated with the battery.

voltage. As illustrated in Tables 9 and 10
, the proposed method has the best evaluation indexes and accurate prediction results at multiple temperatures as −10 °C, 4 °C, 13.5 °C, 20 °C, and 50 °C, indicating good accuracy and robustness.

Fig. 3 (a) to (c) show the overall comparison curves on the left and the local zoomed-in graphs on the right. Fig. 3 (a) compares the proposed method and other methods at −10 °C. The prediction curves of support vector regression (SVR) and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) differ from the true curves, the prediction curve of gate recurrent unit (GRU) performs slightly better than the first two methods. Still, the prediction value deviates from the true value when the voltage is lower than 2.4 V, which proves the inaccuracy of the above comparison methods. The prediction value of designed method has the smallest discrepancy from the true value, and also performs well at low voltages. Fig. 3 (b) compares the prediction values with the dataset of 20 °C battery. The prediction curve of SVR has a significant difference from the real curve, especially at the peak value. The prediction curves of XGBoost and GRU perform a little bit better with the real curve, but these two methods do not realize well at the peaks and valleys. The prediction of our proposed method has the smallest difference and performs well at the peaks and valleys. Fig. 3 (c) compares our method with others on 50 °C. The prediction curve of SVR has a large difference from the true curve, and the overall prediction value is high. XGBoost performs poorly at the peaks and troughs, and GRU has an overall trend similar to the true value, but there is still a gap. All the methods have a gap between the prediction values and the true values at a specific place due to the high temperature, and our method with the smallest gap from the overall point of view.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the prediction curves on 18650 dataset have drawn in the left, and amplification image of the interest area is presented in the right. The predicted results of SVR and GRU are fluctuated greatly and upward drift from the real ones, and the predicted curve of XGBoost skews larger than the truth as the time series grow longer. Our prediction result also has the smallest difference and performs best in this dataset.

In Fig. 4 (a) and (b), the method proposed in this paper achieves accurate prediction of the voltage of the 18650 battery dataset, based on which the network can realize accurate prediction of battery voltage faults. As shown in Fig. 4 (c), the fault prediction model reads the predicted voltage values, determines the current state according to different thresholds, and gives an alarm message. The network is able to accurately detect abnormal voltage values and issue alarms, which proves the feasibility of the fault prediction method proposed in this paper.

To demonstrate our method's effectiveness, an ablation experiment is taken to validate the network. This experiment compares the models in two aspects: the evaluation index of prediction results and the computing time cost, as shown in Table 11 and Table 12 , respectively. The model Bi-LSTM is referred to the classic Bi-LSTM without both the self-attention mechanism and 1DCNN network. The model Bi-LSTM-Attn is served to the classic Bi-LSTM only adding the self-attention mechanism. The model 1DCNN-Bi-LSTM is defined as the classic Bi-LSTM only adding the 1DCNN network, and ours denotes to the proposed network with both the self-attention mechanism and 1DCNN network. As shown in Table 11, our proposed algorithm captures the state of art value, and proves the necessity of our network structure in this paper. As presented in Tables 12 and it proves the 1DCNN network increases the computing efficiency remarkably, and the self-attention mechanism has less influence.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the ablation experiment results at multiple temperatures have been drawn at left and the local zoomed-in graphs at right. Fig. 5 (a)–(c) demonstrate the comparison curves of the ablation experiment at −10 °C, 20 °C and 50 °C, respectively. The curves in Fig. 5 (a) and (c) show that under −10 °C and 50 °C temperature conditions are skewing upward from the real one, perhaps the temperature influence couldn't be considered without self-attention mechanism while the temperature is apart from the battery optimum operating temperature. Fig. 5 (b) demonstrates the comparison curves at 20 °C that are the battery optimum operating temperature, the prediction value falls behind the real one obvious without the 1DCNN network, perhaps is refer to the extract battery feature process. In sum, our proposed algorithm captures the comparing values in the whole prediction time series. The self-attention mechanism and 1DCNN network is necessary for the battery prediction task based on the Bi-LSTM network structure.
and the prediction curves and listed values can prove their effectiveness as shown in Tables 11–12 and Fig. 5 (a)–(c).

For the battery faults prediction issue, a novel method based on temporal neural network is proposed, which is suitable for various temperature conditions with a self-attention mechanism. The network structure contains the 1DCNN, Bi-LSTM and self-attention module. Meanwhile a self-made 18650 battery dataset has been set up at 4 °C and 13.5 °C environment temperature to verify the algorithm's robustness.

The results indicate the proposed algorithm is better than the GRU, XGBoost, SVR at both A123 and 18650 battery datasets under multiple battery temperatures with the quantitative index of MAPE, RMSE, MAE, and R2. The errors in the prediction results of our proposed method for 4 °C and 13.5 °C ambient experiments are about 0.03 % for MAPE, 0.0003 % for RMSE, 0.12 % for MAE, and 0.99 for R2. Furthermore, the ablation experiment indicates that 1DCNN module can save computing consumption and Bi-LSTM structure with self-attention mechanism could increase the prediction accuracy under multi-temperature battery conditions.
However, the practical application of data-driven methods is influenced by computational costs. We aim to further optimize the computational efficiency of the network model in future work.

Yufeng Huang: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xuejian Gong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhiyu Lin: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Lei Xu: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
The public data used was referenced accordingly in the text, the self-made 18650 battery data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and company that could be construed as influencing the position.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported in part by the Shenyang Science and Technology Project under Grant 23-503-6-18, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Liaoning Province (LJ232410143060 and LJ222410143071) and the Key R&D program of Liaoning Province 2023JH2/101300145 and 2024021056-JH2/1025.
